meta.title: Spring Boot
meta.description: Spring Boot applications with dependency injection, JPA, security, and actuators

items.text: Is constructor injection preferred over field injection?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use constructor injection instead of @Autowired on fields. Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit and enables testing.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Field injection hides dependencies, prevents immutability, and makes unit testing harder (requires reflection or Spring context).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for @Autowired on fields. Verify classes use constructor injection (with Lombok @RequiredArgsConstructor or explicit constructors).
baseContent.exampleComment: This service uses @Autowired field injection. Refactor to constructor injection for testability and immutability.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use constructor injection. Use Lombok @RequiredArgsConstructor to reduce boilerplate.

items.text: Are environment-specific configs managed with profiles?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use Spring profiles (application-{profile}.yml) for environment-specific configuration. Don't hardcode environment values.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hardcoded environment values require code changes for deployment. Profiles enable clean separation of dev/staging/prod configs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for hardcoded URLs, credentials, or env-specific values in application.yml. Verify they use profiles or environment variables.
baseContent.exampleComment: The database URL is hardcoded to localhost. Use application-dev.yml for local and environment variables for production.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use profiles for environment-specific config. Use ${ENV_VAR} syntax for secrets.

items.text: Are bean scopes appropriate (singleton vs prototype vs request)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Beans are singleton by default. Stateful beans that shouldn't be shared need @Scope("prototype") or @RequestScope.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Singleton beans with mutable state cause thread-safety issues. Request-scoped beans injected into singletons cause scoping problems.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for mutable fields in singleton beans. Verify request-scoped beans aren't injected directly into singletons.
baseContent.exampleComment: This singleton service has a mutable userContext field. This will be shared across threads. Use @RequestScope or pass context as a parameter.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Singletons must be stateless. Use appropriate scopes for stateful beans.

baseContent.whatItMeans: @ConditionalOnProperty, @ConditionalOnMissingBean, and @Profile control when beans are created. They must be used carefully.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong conditions cause missing beans at runtime (NoSuchBeanDefinitionException) or unexpected behavior in specific environments.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that conditional beans have fallbacks. Verify the conditions match actual deployment scenarios.
baseContent.exampleComment: This bean is @ConditionalOnProperty('feature.x') but there's no default. Add a default value or a fallback bean.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test all conditional paths. Ensure every condition has a clear default behavior.

items.text: Are JPA relationships using appropriate fetch strategies?

baseContent.whatItMeans: @OneToMany defaults to LAZY, @ManyToOne defaults to EAGER. Understand when to override and when to use JOIN FETCH.
baseContent.whyItMatters: EAGER loading on collections loads all related entities always. LAZY loading without fetch joins causes N+1 queries.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check @OneToMany for EAGER (avoid). Check queries accessing LAZY relationships — they need JOIN FETCH or @EntityGraph.
baseContent.exampleComment: This @OneToMany(fetch = EAGER) loads all order items for every order query. Change to LAZY and use JOIN FETCH where needed.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Default to LAZY loading. Use JOIN FETCH or @EntityGraph for specific queries that need related data.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Accessing LAZY relationships in a loop triggers individual queries for each entity. Use @EntityGraph, JOIN FETCH, or batch fetching.
baseContent.whyItMatters: N+1 queries are the most common JPA performance problem. Loading 100 orders with items makes 101 queries instead of 1-2.
baseContent.howToVerify: Enable hibernate.show_sql or use a SQL logger. Check for repeated queries when accessing collections.
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint loads users then accesses user.roles in a loop. Add @EntityGraph(attributePaths = "roles") to the repository method.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always check for N+1 queries. Use @EntityGraph or JPQL JOIN FETCH.

baseContent.whatItMeans: @Transactional should be on service methods, not repositories. Understand propagation, readOnly, and rollback behavior.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong transaction boundaries cause partial updates, LazyInitializationException, or unnecessary locking.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that @Transactional is on service methods that need atomicity. Verify readOnly=true for read-only operations. Check propagation settings.
baseContent.exampleComment: This method reads data then writes. The @Transactional(readOnly=true) will cause the write to fail. Remove readOnly.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Put @Transactional on service methods. Use readOnly=true for queries. Understand propagation.

items.text: Are DTOs or projections used instead of exposing entities?

baseContent.whatItMeans: REST endpoints should return DTOs, not JPA entities. Use Spring Data projections or MapStruct for entity-to-DTO mapping.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Exposing entities leaks internal structure, causes serialization issues with LAZY proxies, and couples API to database schema.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if controllers return entity types directly. Look for @JsonIgnore used to hide fields (sign of missing DTO).
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint returns the User entity directly, exposing password_hash. Create a UserResponse DTO.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never expose JPA entities in API responses. Use DTOs to control what data is returned.

baseContent.whatItMeans: SecurityFilterChain should define which endpoints require authentication. Use .authenticated() for protected routes and .permitAll() for public ones.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Misconfigured security rules can expose admin endpoints to unauthenticated users or lock out legitimate users.
baseContent.howToVerify: Review the SecurityFilterChain configuration. Verify admin endpoints require admin roles. Check that the order of rules is correct (most specific first).
baseContent.exampleComment: The security config permits all requests to /api/**. This includes /api/admin which should require ADMIN role.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Order security rules from most specific to least. Default to authenticated, then whitelist public endpoints.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Database passwords, API keys, and JWT secrets should come from environment variables, Vault, or Spring Cloud Config — not committed in config files.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Secrets in application.yml are visible to anyone with repository access. Leaked database credentials compromise all data.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check application.yml and application.properties for hardcoded credentials. Verify they use ${ENV_VAR} or external config.
baseContent.exampleComment: spring.datasource.password is hardcoded. Use ${DB_PASSWORD} and set it as an environment variable.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Externalize all secrets. Use environment variables or a secret manager.

items.text: Is method-level security used for fine-grained access control?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use @PreAuthorize, @Secured, or @RolesAllowed on service methods for authorization beyond URL-pattern matching.
baseContent.whyItMatters: URL-based security alone can't enforce object-level permissions like 'users can only edit their own resources'.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if sensitive service methods have authorization annotations. Verify @EnableMethodSecurity is configured.
baseContent.exampleComment: This updateUser method allows any authenticated user to update any user. Add @PreAuthorize('#userId == authentication.principal.id').
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use method-level security for object-level authorization. Combine with URL-based security for defense in depth.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Spring Actuator exposes /actuator/health, /actuator/env, /actuator/beans, etc. Sensitive endpoints must be secured or disabled.
baseContent.whyItMatters: /actuator/env exposes all environment variables including secrets. /actuator/heapdump can leak sensitive memory contents.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check which actuator endpoints are exposed. Verify sensitive ones (env, heapdump, configprops) are not publicly accessible.
baseContent.exampleComment: management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=* exposes all actuator endpoints. Limit to health,info and secure the rest.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Only expose health and info publicly. Secure or disable all other actuator endpoints.

items.text: Are request bodies validated with @Valid and Bean Validation?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use @Valid on @RequestBody parameters with Jakarta Bean Validation annotations (@NotNull, @Size, @Email, etc.) on DTOs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without validation, invalid data reaches the service layer, causing unclear errors or data corruption.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check controller methods for @Valid on request body parameters. Verify DTOs have appropriate validation annotations.
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint accepts a CreateUserRequest without @Valid. Add @Valid and @NotBlank/@Email annotations to the DTO fields.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use @Valid with Bean Validation annotations. Handle MethodArgumentNotValidException globally.

items.text: Is there consistent error handling with @ControllerAdvice?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use @ControllerAdvice with @ExceptionHandler to handle exceptions globally. Return consistent error response formats.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without global error handling, different endpoints return different error formats, making client integration difficult.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for a @ControllerAdvice class. Verify it handles common exceptions (validation, not found, unauthorized) with consistent format.
baseContent.exampleComment: This controller catches exceptions inline with try-catch. Move error handling to a @ControllerAdvice for consistency.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use @ControllerAdvice for global error handling. Return a consistent error response DTO.

items.text: Do endpoints return appropriate HTTP status codes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use 201 for creation, 204 for no-content deletes, 400 for validation errors, 404 for not found, 409 for conflicts.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong status codes break client error handling. Returning 200 for errors makes it impossible to distinguish success from failure.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that POST endpoints return 201, DELETE returns 204, and error cases return 4xx/5xx, not 200.
baseContent.exampleComment: This POST endpoint returns 200 on success. Use ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.CREATED) for resource creation.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use semantically correct HTTP status codes. They are part of your API contract.

items.text: Are test slices used instead of full @SpringBootTest where possible?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use @WebMvcTest for controller tests, @DataJpaTest for repository tests, @MockBean for mocking. Full @SpringBootTest is slow.
baseContent.whyItMatters: @SpringBootTest loads the entire application context. Slice tests load only what's needed, running 10-100x faster.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for @SpringBootTest on tests that only test a controller or repository. These should use slice annotations.
baseContent.exampleComment: This controller test uses @SpringBootTest but only tests the UserController. Use @WebMvcTest(UserController.class) instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use the narrowest test slice that covers your test. Save @SpringBootTest for integration tests.

items.text: Are integration tests using Testcontainers or embedded databases?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Integration tests should use Testcontainers for real database testing or H2 for speed. Don't depend on external services.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Tests that depend on external databases are flaky, hard to run locally, and block CI when the database is unavailable.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for hardcoded database URLs in test configs. Verify @Testcontainers or in-memory databases are configured.
baseContent.exampleComment: These tests connect to a shared test database. Use @Testcontainers with PostgreSQLContainer for isolated testing.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Testcontainers for realistic database tests. Use H2 when speed matters more than accuracy.

baseContent.whatItMeans: @MockBean replaces beans in the Spring context. Over-mocking (mocking the class you're testing) makes tests meaningless.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Tests that mock everything verify mock behavior, not actual code. They pass even when the real code is broken.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that @MockBean is only used for external dependencies (APIs, databases). The class under test should not be mocked.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test mocks the service it's supposed to test. Remove the mock and test the actual service with a mocked repository.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Mock external dependencies, not the code under test. Integration tests should use real implementations.
